rulings.Case Title: ITC FINANCIAL LICENSES, INC. VS. STAN SCHULDINER
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Reconsider Order Granting Mtn To Tax Costs
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 30, 2019, Line 1, CROSS DEFENDANTS ITC FINANCIAL LICENSES, INC., THE BANCORP BANK's (INCORRECTLY SUED AS THE BANCORP, INC.) Motion To Reconsider Order Granting Motion To Tax Costs. The hearing will be at 9am, not 9:30am. Cross-defendants ITC Financial Licenses, Inc. and The Bancorp Bank's motion to reconsider the order granting cross-complainant Stan Schuldiner's motion to tax costs is granted. The ITC parties are awarded costs of $6,590.17. The ITC parties have shown that the court should exercise its discretion under both CCP 1008 and the court's inherent authority to reconsider the prior order and, upon such reconsideration, the evidence on this motion shows that the ITC parties are entitled to an award of costs of $6,590.17. The failure to present receipts for the costs claimed on the prior motion has now been remedied and the interests of justice would not be served by now precluding the ITC parties from receiving the costs that they would otherwise have received had it not been for their now remediated error. The December 21, 2018 memorandum of costs filed by the ITC parties claims filing and motion fees of $1,620.70, deposition costs of $5,816.51 and total costs of $7,437.21, which, absent a timely filed amended memorandum, are the maximum amounts of costs recoverable by ITC and Bancorp. There was no timely filed amended memorandum. Assuming without deciding that the change in the website language constitutes recovery "other than monetary relief" by Mr. Schuldiner within the meaning of CCP 1032(a)(4), the court exercises its discretion per that subsection to determine that ITC and Bancorp are the prevailing parties for purposes of recovery of costs because the primary relief sought by Mr. Schuldiner was monetary, not injunctive. ITC and Bancorp have shown that they reasonably and necessarily incurred at least $1,620.70 in filing and motion fees and $4,969.47 in deposition costs for a total of $6,590.17, which will be added to the judgment in their favor. rulings.Case Title: ANDRIY KRAVCHUK VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion And Notice Of Hearing Re Plaintiff Motion To Amend Judgment Based On Clerical Error
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 30, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFF ARTEM KOSHKALDA's Motion And Notice Of Hearing Re Plaintiff Motion To Amend Judgment Based On Clerical Error. Plaintiff's motion to amend judgment based on clerical error is granted in part. The Court's June 12, 2019 Judgment contained a typographical error that referred to the filing date of the City's memorandum of costs as May 4, 2018 rather than May 6, 2019. Pursuant to the Court's inherent power to correct clerical errors in a judgment at any time, the judgment is amended to reflect the correct filing date. Plaintiff's motion is otherwise denied. Although the Court denied the City's motion for an award of attorneys' fees under Code of Civil Procedure section 1038, the City is independently entitled under section 1032(b) to recover its costs as a matter of right as a prevailing party. The record demonstrates that defendant filed and served the costs memorandum on May 6, 2019, and plaintiff never filed a motion to tax costs in compliance with California Rule of Court 3.1700, which provides that a notice of motion to strike or to tax costs must be served and filed 15 days after service of the cost memorandum. Plaintiff's failure to file a timely motion to tax costs waived any objections. (Santos v. Civil Service Bd. (1987) 193 Cal.App.3d 1442, 1447 ["failure to file a motion to tax costs constitutes a waiver of the right to object"].) Plaintiff's reference to costs in his brief in opposition to the City's fees motion did not relieve him of the obligation to comply with Rule 3.1700. rulings.Case Title: NADIA CHAN VS. HAL EUGENE ROWLAND ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Staying Discovery In Civil Proceeding Pending Disposition Of Criminal Proceeding
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 30, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT HAL ROWLAND Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Staying Discovery In Civil Proceeding Pending Disposition Of Criminal Proceeding. Defendant Hal Eugene Rowland's motion for an order staying discovery pending disposition of criminal proceeding is denied. On May 23, 2019, Defendant entered a no contest plea to two criminal charges (one felony and one misdemeanor) and the court made a finding of guilt. By the terms of the deferred entry of judgment (DEJ) to which Defendant agreed, on November 22, 2019, if Defendant complies with specified terms and conditions, judgment will be entered and sentence imposed on the basis of the misdemeanor plea; if he is in violation of those terms, judgment will be entered and sentence imposed on the felony. The terms and conditions require that Defendant not suffer any new arrests or violate the stay away order. Thus, although the criminal matter is not yet finally disposed of, Defendant has been convicted and found guilty, and has not shown any substantial risk that any incriminating testimony he may give in this civil case will be used against him in criminal court in violation of his Fifth Amendment rights. Upon weighing the competing interests of the parties, the court determines that Defendant has not shown good cause to stay discovery. (See Keating v. Office of Thrift Supervision (9th Cir. 1995) 45 F.3d 322, 324; Avant! Corp. v. Superior Court (2000) 79 Cal.App.4th 876, 886; Pacers, Inc. v. Superior Court (2005) 162 Cal.App.3d 686, 688-689.) rulings.Case Title: BANK OF AMERICA, N.A VS. YELENA A DELAQUIZE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Order That Matters In Request For Admission Of Truth Of Acts Be Admitted; Memo Iso; Declaration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Tuesday, July 30, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF BANK OF AMERICA, N.A.'S Motion For Order That Matters In Request For Admission Of Truth Of Acts Be Admitted. Pro Tem Judge Paul Renne, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: NO OPPOSITION TO THIS MOTION HAS BEEN FILED, ACCORDINGLY THE COURT WILL ISSUE THE FOLLOWING ORDER: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION FOR AN ORDER THAT MATTERS IN REQUESTS FOR ADMISSION OF TRUTH OF FACTS BE ADMITTED IS GRANTED AS FOLLOWS: 1. DEFENDANT YELENA A. DELAQUIZE MUST SERVE WITHIN TEN (10) DAYS FROM THE DATE OF THIS ORDER FULL AND COMPLETE CODE COMPLIANT RESPONSES, WITHOUT OBJECTION, TO EACH REQUESTS FOR ADMISSION. 2. IF DEFENDANT FAILS TO COMPLY WITH PARAGRAPH (1) ABOVE WITHIN TEN (10) DAYS FROM THE DATE OF THIS ORDER THE TRUTH OF ALL FACTS SHALL BE DEEMED ADMITTED AND DEFENDANT WILL BE PRECLUDED FROM ANY ATTEMPT TO CHALLENGE THE TRUTH OF THE ADMISSIONS. 3. NO MONETARY SANCTIONS WERE SOUGHT AND NONE WILL BE IMPOSED. PLAINTFF HAS INDICATED THAT IT WILL NOT MAKE AN APPEARANCE AS PROVIDED FOR BY CALIF. RULES OF COURT SEC. 3.1304(c). COUNSEL IS DIRECTED TO PREPARE AND SUBMIT ELECTRONICALLY TO THE COURT AN ORDER CONSISTENT WITH THIS TENTATIVE RULING FOR EXCUTION AND FILING. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rennepar85@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling AND SUBMIT ELECTRONICALLY TO THE COURT AN ORDER CONSISTENT WITH THIS TENTATIVE RULING FOR EXCUTION AND FILING. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JANICE FROST et al VS. ALDON J SMITH et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 30, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT ALDON SMITH's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendant Aldon J. Smith's motion to strike punitive damages in Plaintiffs Janice Frost and Sean Frost's complaint is denied. The allegations of negligent entrustment in the complaint are sufficient to support the punitive damages prayer. Because negligent entrustment is a negligence claim, there can be no finding of "malice" to support punitive damages without allegations showing intent to perform an act known to have a high probability of harm. (See Flores v. Enterprise Rent-A-Car Co. (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 1055, 1063 [reversing denial of summary adjudication of negligent entrustment claim and punitive damages request where there was no evidence the defendant was impaired or otherwise unfit to drive when the vehicle was rented to him and statute circumscribed duty of rental car company to research DMV records of driver]; Truong v. Nguyen (2007) 156 Cal.App.4th 865, 875 ["Liability for negligent entrustment is determined by applying general principles of negligence…"]; Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, 895-896 ["In order to justify an award of punitive damages on this basis, the plaintiff must establish that the defendant was aware of the probable dangerous consequences of his conduct, and that he wilfully and deliberately failed to avoid those consequences."].) Yet, while negligence, gross negligence, or even recklessness on their own are insufficient to form a basis for punitive damages under Civil Code section 3294, allegations showing conscious disregard of the rights and safety of others can give rise to punitive damages. (See G.D. Searle & Co. v. Superior Court (1975) 49 Cal.App.3d 22, 28-29, 32 [conscious disregard of safety may justify an exemplary damage award when nondeliberate injury is alleged].) Here, Plaintiffs allege that Defendant Smith knowingly allowed Defendant Hardy to drive a motor vehicle while she was under the influence of alcohol and impaired, and that he knew she could seriously injure or kill others as a result and that it was highly probable that harm to others could result. Knowledge of a high probability of harm emerges if the one who entrusts his vehicle knows the would-be driver is intoxicated. The principle announced in Taylor that willfully subjecting the public to the dangers of drunk driving activity can give rise to punitive damages may logically be extended to the person who hands the driver the keys while aware of the driver's impairment. (See Taylor, 24 Cal.3d at 897 [willful decision to combine "sharply impaired physical and mental faculties with a vehicle capable of great force and speed, reasonably may be held to exhibit a conscious disregard of the safety of others" regardless of whether "the driver had a prior history of drunk driving incidents"].) The degree of the vehicle owner's knowledge and the likelihood of the harm are factual issues better resolved at later stages of the proceedings after discovery. rulings.Case Title: JANICE FROST et al VS. ALDON J SMITH et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 30, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANTS ALDON SMITH, SHANICE HARDY MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendant Shanice M. Hardy's motion to strike portions of Plaintiffs Janice Frost and Sean Frost's complaint is denied. The allegations that Defendant was driving unsafely while under the influence of alcohol are sufficient to support Plaintiffs' claim for punitive damages. (Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, 899 ["one who voluntarily commences, and thereafter continues, to consume alcoholic beverages to the point of intoxication, knowing from the outset that he must thereafter operate a motor vehicle demonstrates . . . 'such a conscious and deliberate disregard of the interests of others that his conduct may be called willful or wanton'"] [holding that trial court erred in sustaining demurrer to portion of complaint which sought recovery of punitive damages].) Defendant's attempt to distinguish Taylor and other similar cases on their facts is unpersuasive. Taylor held that while "aggravating factors" such as prior arrests and misconduct "may heighten the probability and foreseeability of an accident," they are not "essential prerequisites to the assessment of punitive damages in drunk driving cases." (Taylor, 24 Cal.3d at 896; see also id. at 897 ["One who willfully consumes alcoholic beverages to the point of intoxication, knowing that he thereafter must operate a motor vehicle, thereby combining sharply impaired physical and mental faculties with a vehicle capable of great force and speed, reasonably may be held to exhibit a conscious disregard of the safety of others. The effect may be lethal whether or not the driver had a prior history of drunk driving incidents"].) The essential gravamen of the complaint in Taylor was that "Defendant became intoxicated and thereafter drove a car while in that condition, despite his knowledge of the safety hazard he created thereby." (Id. at 896.) Plaintiff's allegations against Defendant are to the same effect. Dawes v. Superior Court (1980) 111 Cal.App.3d 82 reversed an order striking punitive damages allegations, concluding that allegations of running a stop sign, speeding, and zigzagging in a crowded area while intoxicated sufficiently stated a basis for punitive damages. (Id. at 88-89.) However, Dawes did not displace the threshold for pleading punitive damages in drunk driving cases established in Taylor; it merely concluded that there was no basis to strike allegations that went beyond the baseline set by Taylor. (See Dawes, 111 Cal.App.3d at 89-90.) Taylor was not overruled or disapproved following the 1987 amendments to Civil Code section 3294 that added the "despicable conduct" element to requests for punitive damages. (See College Hospital Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Taylor remains good law and compels this Court to find that Plaintiff's allegation of drunk driving is sufficient to withstand a motion to strike at the pleading stage. Whether there is sufficient evidence to ultimately support an award of punitive damages must be decided after Plaintiff has had a chance to conduct discovery. rulings.Case Title: INGRID ELIZABETH DE LEON RODAS VS. FCA US LLC ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, July 30, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT FCA US LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY, MICHAEL STEAD'S AUTO DEPOT, INC. A CALIFORNIA AMENDED DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendants FCA US LLC and Michael Stead's Auto Depot, Inc.'s demurrer to the fourth cause of action for fraudulent inducement/concealment is sustained without leave to amend. The economic loss rule bars the claim. Plaintiffs allege that Defendants concealed certain defects in the electrical architecture of their vehicles, but does not allege that their vehicle was affected by any of the problems allegedly caused by those electrical defects, nor do they plead that they suffered any damages other than the alleged economic damage related to the purchase of the vehicle itself. The defect of which Plaintiffs complain was a transmission/shifting problem, not an electrical problem. (Compl. 175-178.) Plaintiffs also fail to plead any facts showing that she was exposed to liability for personal damages independent of the economic loss. "The economic loss rule requires a purchaser to recover in contract for purely economic loss due to disappointed expectations, unless he can demonstrate harm above and beyond a broken contractual promise." (Robinson Helicopter Co., Inc. v. Dana Corp. (2004) 34 Cal.4th 979, 988; Food Safety net Services v. Eco Safe Systems USA, Inc. (2012) 209 Cal.App.4th 1118, 1132 [economic loss rule barred fraud claim based on falsehoods that were not conceptually distinct from the contract].) Plaintiffs' reliance on the "narrow" exception to the rule in Robinson Helicopter Co. is misplaced, as the Court's holding in that case was "limited to a defendant's affirmative misrepresentations on which a plaintiff relies and which expose a plaintiff to liability for personal damages independent of the plaintiff's economic loss." (34 Cal.4th at 993.) As Plaintiffs concede, they do not allege that Defendants made any affirmative misrepresentations.
